## Abstract

In this study we presented generalized exponential power distribution as an alternative to normal distribution commonly used in the analysis of agricultural data. The distribution which is more robust in modeling because of the present of shape parameters, which regulates it tails. Some of its mathematical and statistical properties are examined. The application of the probability density function is demonstrated in fitting poultry feeds data. The goodness-of-fit test was carried to show that it is a better substitute to normal distribution in applications.

### Keywords

## Author Information

#### Akinlolu Adeseye Olosunde \*

\*Address all correspondence to: akinolosunde@gmail.com

## 1. Introduction

Cholesterol is a waxy substance that comes from two main sources: the liver and food intake. It has been noted that high level of cholesterol can block the arteries, decrease blood flow to other tissue in the body, thereby causing heart diseases [[1](#B1)]. Eggs have commonly been jettisoned owing to high cholesterol contents, therefore lowering consumption rate. But [[1](#B1)] further note that eggs are high quality source protein and other nutrients. It is also well known that eggs contain lecithin and phospholipids, necessary for the construction of brain cell membrane. In terms of feeding intellect, their value lies mainly in the quality of their proteins, they are actually rich in amino acids, essential in the production of the principal neurotransmitters. Hence, instead of a total boycott, it is better to reduce the risk associated with eggs consumption.

In order to alleviate the problem associated with consumption, an organic copper salt combination was used instead of the inorganic combinations currently used in preparation of poultry feeds. Ninety-six chickens were randomly selected and randomly divided into 2 groups of 48 each. Each group was subjected to the same general conditions and treatments, differing only in that while, the chickens in the first group were fed with inorganic copper salt, those in the second group were fed with inorganic copper salt. After 4 months the weight (gram) and cholesterol level (mg/egg) of the eggs yielded by the two groups were measured. The excerpt from the data used in this analysis is presented in the Appendix A courtesy of Federal University of Agriculture, Abeokuta.

Carrying out analysis of the data obtained from the laboratory study, it is a common assumption in literature to assume normal distribution in the analysis of agricultural data. This assumption is not always true especially in the current case. Therefore, we proposed to study a generalized form of normal distribution called the exponential power distribution and its multivariate extension, which contains normal distribution and others in the literature as special cases in fitting poultry feeds data. This unifying exponential power distribution is characterized by a parameter 

β
 and a function 

h

β
 which regulates the tail behaviour of the distribution, thus making it more flexible and suitable for modeling than the usual normal distribution, while retaining symmetry properties. Finally we fit the generalized exponential power distribution as well as the normal distribution to data on eggs produced by chicken on each of two different poultry feeds (inorganic and organic copper salt compositions) and show that the generalized exponential power distribution fit is considerably better. We then use the Kolmogorov-Smirnov two samples one-tailed test to show that there is an increase in egg weights and decrease in cholesterol level when the feed is organic.

## 2. Exponential power distribution

The exponential power distribution is a class of densities which includes the normal and allows thick tails, Thus making it more suitable in modeling when compared with the usual normal distribution. In fact, it is a natural generalization of the normal distribution and also used in applications by [[2](#B2), [3](#B3), [4](#B4), [5](#B5), [6](#B6), [7](#B7), [8](#B8)]. They also presented a multivariate version of the exponential power distribution and [[5](#B5)] used this distribution to model repeated measurements. We present this distribution with a proposition. Codes were written in R environment to estimate the parameters of both the univariate and the multivariate version of the distribution see [[7](#B7), [9](#B9), [10](#B10)].

## 3. Data analysis

Normality assumptions are common in data analysis, but a close look at the normal and generalized exponential power Q-Q plot in [Figures 1](#F1) and [2](#F2), though by the normal distribution is a reasonable fit, the tails seem to be shorter than expected and hence the *p*-values resulting from the usual tests based on normal assumptions cannot be trusted. On the other hand, the generalized exponential power distribution proves to be a much better fit for egg weights as well as cholesterol level in both groups. We now carry out estimation of the parameters of the distribution using the method of moments and maximum likelihood estimate (MLE). These methods were preferred because they have many optimal properties in estimation: sufficiency; consistency; efficiency; and parametrization invariance which are rarely found in other approaches (For details on MLE see [[4](#B4), [9](#B9), [10](#B10)] and the estimated values from the observations namely the means, standard deviation (s.d.), the

The statistical computing environment [[10](#B10)], was supplemented with the package called “normp” downloaded into R file from the site <http://cran.r-project.org>/ and used to analyze the data. As earlier stated, though the normal distribution was a good fit to the sample data, but we have a better fit when we use the generalized exponential power distribution. ### 3.1 Hypothesis testing

SUMMARY:
Decisions regarding significant differences between diverse test ingredients and the reference in a dose–response study aimed at estimating the RBV of a nutrient could be misleading in the absence of CIs. In animal studies, we use _y_ = _f_(_x_) to predict response from doses, as discussed in [59]. The identified pitfalls can be summarized as follows: _1_) failure to estimate RBV and reliance on a simple division operation; _2_) neglecting the estimation of the common intercept and using the mean response of the control diet as the intercept in the models; _3_) using separate linear equations for reference and test diets based on the levels of the test nutrient; _4_) overlooking critical statistical indices, such as CIs and _P_-values of the model.

Abstract
--------

Assessing nutrients’ relative bioavailability value (RBV) in poultry nutrition has been a prominent subject in the scientific literature for several decades. This method of nutritional evaluation is commonly used to appraise emerging sources of trace minerals and amino acid chelates. References outlining the method for estimating RBV have been available since the 1970s. However, a simplified approach to RBV estimation using the slope-ratio method without preceding statistical considerations to ensure validity and meet fundamental requirements may yield misleading conclusions. Using the slope-ratio method, which involves dividing the regression slope of the test ingredient by that of the reference, can cause uncertainties regarding statistical significance if the model’s probability is reported without confidence intervals (CIs) for the RBV estimates. Despite longstanding criticism regarding the misinterpretation and improper use of statistical tests and CIs, these issues persist in estimating RBV using the slope-ratio method. An additional concern is that the misuse of the slope-ratio method and the application of inappropriate statistical analyses can lead to the underestimation of the RBV of nutrients in poultry species. This means that improper application of these methods can cause inaccurately low RBV values, affecting the assessment of nutrient effectiveness. This review addresses the potential pitfalls in peer-reviewed articles within this field, with a particular focus on zinc bioavailability through a reevaluation of RBV data in broilers, laying hens, and honeybees.

Introduction
------------

The relative bioavailability value (RBV) in poultry production is pivotal, denoting the proportion of nutrients from a test ingredient the body absorbs and uses compared with nutrients from a reference source [1], mainly using slope-ratio assays were developed for dilutions of the same material, not materials with different activities [[2], [3], [4]]. Precise RBV estimates are essential for formulating poultry diets that fulfill nutritional requirements, mitigate nutrient excretion, and minimize environmental impacts. Nevertheless, inaccurate RBV estimates carry risk of either under- or over-supplementing nutrients, potentially leading to heightened nutrient excretion and environmental pollution, or conversely, nutrient deficiency and compromised performance.

Although this study is focused on the statistical issues surrounding RBV estimation, the nutrient zinc (Zn) was chosen for reanalysis due to the availability of comprehensive data on Zn in broilers, laying hens, and honeybees. This selection is not meant to single out Zn as the primary focus, but rather to provide a detailed case study illustrating broader methodological principles that can be applied to other nutrients.

Zn is a crucial mineral with a significant impact on animals’ growth, development, and immune function, including broilers and laying hens. Deficiency in Zn can give rise to diverse health issues, encompassing growth retardation, compromised immune function, and reproductive failure [5]. The bioavailability of Zn in animal diets is subject to various factors, including the form in which Zn is present, the composition of other dietary components, the age of the animal, and its health status [6].

Commonly used Zn sources in poultry diets include oxide and sulfate forms, with the latter noted for their higher bioavailability [7]. Using organic Zn, such as Zn-methionine (Zn-Met), has enhanced Zn bioavailability in broiler breeders [8]. The methionine hydroxyl analog chelate Zn is chemically more stable than other organic Zn forms and exhibits increased bioavailability in broilers [9]. Studies have indicated that Zn from organic sources can contribute to improved antioxidant enzyme activity and reduced chickens’ malondialdehyde levels. This, in turn, can lead to enhanced overall health, productivity, and a decreased susceptibility to specific health issues [10]. Undoubtedly, meticulous assessments of mineral bioavailability and other essential nutrients, such as amino acids, plays a pivotal role in optimizing poultry diets, reducing nutrient excretion, and mitigating environmental pollution. However, the effectiveness of these strategies may be compromised if the nutrients in the feed ingredients lack high bioavailability. This concern can be exacerbated by underestimating nutrients’ RBV.

This article explores statistical issues related to RBV estimation, such as the lack of a statistical evaluation of data prior to RBV computation, the omission of confidence intervals (CIs) for RBV estimates, and the improper use of the slope-ratio method for RBV estimation without an iterative process. The current study involves reevaluating RBV data for broilers, laying hens, and honeybees, and compares the newly estimated RBV values with those previously reported.

As detailed in previous discussions [11], ensuring accurate measurements of bioavailability assays necessitates the execution of validity checks. These checks encompass the assessment of statistical validity (linearity), fundamental validity (intersection of the regression lines at 0 supplemental dose), and equality of the common intercept to the mean of the blanks. Regrettably, as highlighted in Table 1 [12–55], a significant portion of peer-reviewed articles have overlooked the essential step of statistically validating the data prior to RBV estimation.

The initial and crucial step in data validation is assessing statistical validity, specifically linearity. This check aims to evaluate the statistical relationship between levels of supplementation and the response variable (e.g., growth rate or bone ash). The examination involves scrutinizing the linearity of the relationship, indicating that as supplementation levels increase, the response variable should consistently and proportionally change. Statistical techniques such as analysis of variance using procedure of generalized linear model (PROC GLM) in SAS software are commonly used to assess linearity. If the relationship is not linear, this might suggest issues with the assay or the necessity for further experimental design adjustments, as noted by Finney [11].

The fundamental validity check centers on the intersection point of regression lines at a 0 supplemental level in bioavailability assays [11]. This point represents the baseline or control condition where no supplementation is applied. The regression lines from different treatments or experimental groups are expected to intersect at 0 points. The absence of this intersection suggests a potential bias or systematic error in the assay, necessitating further investigation or adjustments to the experimental design, as noted by Littell et al. [1]. In cases where the lines cannot intersect at 0, the use of a nonlinear model may be more suitable than a linear model. The SAS procedure Nonlinear (NLIN) [56], as discussed by Littell et al. [57], can be used for such nonlinear modeling situations.

The third preconditional assumption involves ensuring the equality of the common intercept of the regression model to the mean of the blank (mean response at 0 supplemental doses). In this type of assay, the relationship between a quantitative response denoted as “y,” a “Standard” preparation dose, represented as _x_ _s_, and a “Test” dose denoted as _x_ _t_ is fitted to a linear equation. The 2 regression lines, derived from experimental data, should intersect at a common point, corresponding to the response values of the “control” or “blank” doses, as elucidated by Finney [11]. However, it has been observed sometimes that the intercept of the regression line is incorrectly considered as the blank or control [[12], [13], [14]]. In other words, the intercept should be estimated by multiple regression and then statistically compared with the blank, meeting the fundamental requirement proposed by Littell et al. [1].

The accuracy of RBV estimation using the slope-ratio method hinges on fulfilling assumptions related to linearity and intersection tests. If these assumptions are not met, the resulting RBV estimate may lack precision, potentially leading to inaccurate conclusions regarding the nutrient’s bioavailability [58]. If the method’s validity has not been adequately assessed, the researcher may lack confidence in the obtained RBV estimates. When the method’s validity has not been adequately assessed, the researcher may need more confidence in the obtained RBV estimates. Such uncertainty can result in misinterpretations of the data and potential misuse of the information in subsequent research or practical applications [11,59]. The absence of adequate statistical validation may contribute to heightened uncertainty in the estimated RBV values. This increased uncertainty makes it more challenging to draw precise conclusions about the nutrient’s bioavailability [11,58]. Moreover, without a thorough check for statistical validity, the researcher may need help to detect significant differences between nutrient levels or treatments. This limitation could result in incorrect conclusions about the nutrient’s effects on the studied parameter [58,59] because proper statistical analysis is crucial when evaluating RBV data to ensure that the results are statistically valid and reliable. This includes checking assumptions, selecting appropriate statistical tests, and accounting for sources of variability.

Inaccurate RBV estimates (i.e., over- or under-estimates) can significantly affect animal nutrition and health, leading to various biological and economic consequences. The RBV estimates are pivotal in determining the appropriate levels of nutrient supplementation in animal diets. Inaccurate and underestimated RBV may result in excessive nutrient supplementation, causing imbalances, reduced feed intake, impaired nutrient utilization, and an increased risk of toxicity. Conversely, an overestimated RBV may lead to inadequate nutrient supplementation, causing deficiencies and associated health issues [[60], [61], [62]]. Under or over-feeding can lead to suboptimal nutrient intake and utilization, impairing growth rates, delaying maturation [60,61], increasing susceptibility to infections, reducing disease resistance, and impairing overall health [8,61,[63], [64], [65], [66]]. Optimal reproductive performance in livestock and poultry depends on accurate nutrient bioavailability. Inaccurate RBV estimates can negatively impact fertility, embryonic development, and reproductive success. Misestimating the RBV of critical micronutrients such as Zn or selenium (Se) may lead to inadequate levels, affecting reproductive outcomes [61,[67], [68], [69]].

The standard error of estimate (SEE) quantifies the accuracy of an individual coefficient estimate within a regression model [70]. This accuracy metric, commonly referred to as the root mean square error, is determined by taking the square root of the average squared deviation. The SEE measures how much the predicted values differ from the actual values and is closely linked to the sum of the squared residuals. It is calculated from the average of these squared deviations and represents the standard deviation of the residuals in a regression model. The SEE serves as an indicator of the precision of the regression coefficients, reflecting the average variation of observed values from predicted values. A smaller SEE suggests that the observed values are closer to the predicted values, indicating a potentially better fit. However, interpreting the SEE value requires considering the study’s context, the scale of the dependent variable, and the objectives of the regression analysis. It is crucial for assessing the accuracy of predictions and serves as a key indicator of how precisely a sample statistic estimates the corresponding population parameter [71]. The SEE measures the variability within the sampling distribution of a statistic, providing an estimate of the likely accuracy of a value based on the sample size. Essentially, the SEE helps evaluate how well the prediction equation aligns with the sample data by examining the dispersion of predicted values from actual values [72].

Mathematically, SEE, in the context of regression analysis, estimates the variability of the residual, the difference between observed and predicted values:S E E=∑(y−y ˆ 2)n−(k+1)

Because n observations are used to estimate k + 1 parameters, we have n − (k + 1) degrees of freedom. Once a confidence level is selected, such as 95%, the value of _t∗_ corresponding to this probability is determined using the _t_ distribution with [_n_ – (_k_ + 1)] degrees of freedom. It is crucial to emphasize that RBV is an estimate and its reporting should be associated with SEE as a statistical measure of accuracy. Unfortunately, it has been observed that some authors have often overlooked this parameter when reporting RBV in animal studies. RBV was derived by simply dividing the slope of the test by the slope of the reference as a calculation value instead of an estimated value in reports by Sahraei and Janmohammadi [15], Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12], Behjatian-Esfahani et al. [13], Farhadi Javid et al. [14]. In addition, the others who estimated RBV using an iterative process did not report the SEE associated with RBV (Table 1).

CIs are a crucial statistical tool used to quantify the uncertainty associated with the estimation of RBV obtained using the slope-ratio method in animal studies. This method involves comparing the slopes of dose–response curves of a nutrient in test and reference animals to estimate its relative bioavailability. However, these estimates are subject to variability due to inter-animal variability, experimental error, and sampling variability. CIs provide a range within which the actual value of RBV will probably fall with a certain level of confidence. For example, a 95% CI indicates that if multiple studies were conducted under similar conditions and each study calculated its own CI, ∼95% of those intervals would contain the true RBV.

The importance of CIs for RBV cannot be overlooked. They provide a more realistic and meaningful interpretation of the results than just reporting point estimates or reporting statistical significance [73]. CIs also help to make informed decisions regarding regulatory approval, clinical trial design, and product development. For instance, if the CI for RBV includes 100%, it suggests no significant difference between the test and reference products, which may lead to regulatory approval without further testing [16]. If the CI (95% CI) does not include 100%, this indicates a statistically significant difference between the products. However, further investigation may be necessary to assess whether this difference is clinically significant (Figure 1).

Decisions regarding significant differences between diverse test ingredients and the reference in a dose–response study aimed at estimating the RBV of a nutrient could be misleading in the absence of CIs. In animal studies, we use _y_ = _f_(_x_) to predict response from doses, as discussed in [59]. The NLIN procedure of SAS [56] can produce an inverse matrix that contains SEs for the estimated parameters, including slopes. This matrix is crucial for calculating the SEs of the ratios derived from these slopes. To compute CIs for the ratios of the estimated parameters (e.g., slopes), the delta method can be applied. This involves using the SEs of the slopes to derive the variance of the ratio, which can then be used to construct the CIs. Dependence solely on the _P_ value of the model might result in inaccurate conclusions. The CI of the RBV estimate is the critical statistic in most studies focusing on RBV estimation in animal studies that must be addressed in various peer-reviewed articles, as shown in Table 1. Various factors can influence the width of CIs for RBV and understanding why they are wide may require a detailed examination of the study design, data, and statistical methods. Here are several plausible reasons why CIs for RBV might be wide:

Small samples highlight how small sample sizes can compromise the internal and external validity of a study, emphasizing the importance of adequate sample sizes for reliable research outcomes [74]. Indrayan and Mishra [75] stated that sample size directly influences research findings, with very small samples undermining study validity and very large samples potentially skewing results. Faber and Fonseca [76] also showed how sample size influences research outcomes, explaining that larger samples lead to narrower CIs, providing more precise estimates, whereas smaller samples result in broader intervals with increased uncertainty and potential for error.

High variability in bioavailability measurements can contribute to wider CIs. Variability might be due to biological differences among subjects, measurement error, or other sources of variability. For example, a study of theophylline bioavailability found that clearance of theophylline is inherently more variable within individuals [coefficient of variation (CV), 13%] than the volume of distribution (8%) [77]. Similarly, a study on intraindividual variability in absolute bioavailability and clearance of midazolam in healthy individuals found that the mean CV (%) for absolute bioavailability and clearance of midazolam was 26 ± 15% and 20 ± 10%, respectively [78]. These sources collectively emphasize the impact of variability in bioavailability measurements and its influence on the width of CIs.

If there is substantial heterogeneity among the subjects or experimental conditions in a study, this can lead to wider CIs. This heterogeneity can introduce additional sources of variation that affect the precision of the RBV estimate. Studies have shown that very small samples can compromise the internal and external validity of a study due to heterogeneity, emphasizing the need for adequate sample sizes for reliable research outcomes [79]. Additionally, variability in bioavailability measurements can also contribute to wider CIs, with factors such as biological differences among subjects, measurement error, and other sources of variability playing a significant role in influencing the precision of estimates [80].

As Littell et al. [1] highlighted, simplifying the slope-ratio method can lead to deceptive conclusions. This simplification, involving the straightforward division of 2 slopes without using an iterative process for estimating the RBV along with SEE and CIs, may result in an underestimation of RBV. The identified pitfalls can be summarized as follows: _1_) failure to estimate RBV and reliance on a simple division operation; _2_) neglecting the estimation of the common intercept and using the mean response of the control diet as the intercept in the models; _3_) using separate linear equations for reference and test diets based on the levels of the test nutrient; _4_) overlooking critical statistical indices, such as CIs and _P_-values of the model. Despite the critical importance of statistically testing the data before estimating the RBV, most of the literature did not undertake the critical steps indicated by Littell et al. [1] before estimating RBV, as shown in Table 1. An outline of the proper examination of the RBV in an animal study is provided in Figure 2.

In the present review, these issues have been illustrated by examining reported data from selected peer-reviewed articles as examples, reporting the RBV of Zn in broilers [14], laying hens [12], and bees [13].

The author conducted a comprehensive literature search using multiple databases and relevant keywords, including RBV, slope-ratio, and exponential regression. The search aimed to capture a wide range of studies on the RBV across different animal models, including rats, pigs, ducks, broilers, laying hens, and honeybees. We acknowledge that the original articles reanalyzed in this study did not include an open data statement. The data for our reanalysis were derived from the mean values reported by the authors in the respective publications. This classification aims to highlight the extent to which each study adhered to best practices in statistical analysis. The following details explain the criteria used to classify the studies listed in Table 1 and the steps taken to ensure an unbiased representation:
*   1)All included studies used the slope-ratio (or exponential) method to estimate the RBV. 
*   2)Studies were assessed to determine if they conducted validity tests prior to RBV estimation to ensure the appropriateness of their statistical models. 
*   3)Using a common intercept in the statistical models was evaluated to ensure consistency of baseline measurements across different studies. 
*   4)Studies were classified based on whether they used multiple regression analysis for RBV estimation, allowing for the consideration of multiple variables and interactions. 
*   5)Studies were assessed to report the SE of the RBV estimates, providing an indication of the precision of these estimates. 
*   6)The classification included an assessment of whether studies reported the 95% CI of RBV estimates, which indicates the range within which the true RBV value is expected to lie with a 95% CI. 
*   7)Studies were evaluated whether they conducted a statistical evaluation of the significance of their RBV estimates based on the 95% CI, ensuring that the reported RBV values were statistically significant. 

The primary technical issue identified in the reported RBV within the selected articles revolved around fitting distinct linear regression lines for test and reference ingredients without using a unified equation covering both components. For instance, Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12] presented 2 separate equations for Zn-Thr (test; _y_ = 1.6321 _x_ + 39.67; _R_ 2 = 0.76) and ZnO (reference; _y_ = 0.6458 _x_ + 39.67; _R_ 2 = 0.63), each accompanied by relevant _R_ 2 values, and then calculating RBV of Zn for internal egg Zn concentration as: 1.6321/0.6458 = 253% relative to ZnO.

The second concern pertained to the intercept values used in the equations. As evident in the equations above, the intercept value was derived from the internal Zn concentration of the eggs in the control group, as indicated by Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12]. This approach needs to be revised, as the model should estimate the linear regression intercept, and this estimated value can vary from the response observed in the control group.

A consistent pattern was observed in all responses to estimate the RBV of Zn across the 3 selected articles, Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12], Behjatian-Esfahani et al. [13], and Farhadi Javid et al. [14], in which the fundamental issues mentioned above were observed, including lack of statistical test for linearity, lack of common intercept, lack of multiple regression, missing the SE and CI of the RBV estimates, and lack of statistical significance test by 95% CI. Upon reusing the data to estimate the RBV of Zn through the iterative process in SAS [56], distinct RBV values for Zn were unveiled in broilers, laying hens, and bees.

The RBV of the test sources was determined by the multivariate linear regression model (Figure 3) using the following equation in SAS [56]:y=a+b t x t+b s x s+e Where _y_ = response criterion, _a_ = common intercept, _b_ _t_ and _b_ _s_ = the slope of the test sources of Zn and the standard, respectively, _x_ _t_ and _x_ _s_ = dietary inclusion level of the test Zn and standard Zn, respectively, _e_ = error. When compared with the standard, the RBV of the test was calculated as the ratio of their linear slopes (_b_ _t_/_b_ _s_ × 100) as described by Littell et al. [57].

As depicted in Figure 4A [[12], [13], [14]], upon reanalyzing the data reported by Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12], it was found that the RBV of Zn-Thr and Zn-Met was estimated at 291% (24 to 559%) and 345% (24 to 665%), respectively, which were statistically higher than those reported by Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12] based on a 2-tailed _t_-test. The common intercept of the multiple regression was calculated as 40.61 (SE = 1.10) with an _R_ 2 value of 0.93. A 1-sample _t_-test analysis (Figure 4K) indicated significant differences between the estimated RBV for Zn-Thr (_P_ = 0.044) and Zn-Met (_P_ = 0.049) compared with the RBV calculated (but NOT estimated) by Behjatian Esfahani et al. [12].

In Figure 4B, the reestimated RBV for Zn-Thr in honeybees was determined to be 314% (−189 to 817%), and the common intercept was calculated as 68.29 (SE = 5.47) with an _R_ 2 value of 0.86. A 1-sample _t_-test suggested that the RBV calculated for Zn-Thr by Behjatian-Esfahani et al. [13] might have tended to underestimate (_P_ = 0.122; Figure 4M).

The RBV of Zn-Thr relative to Zn-sulfate in broilers, considering body weight gain (Gain; Figure 4C), feed conversion ratio (FCR; Figure 4D), ash in the tibia (Figure 4E), Zn in tibia (Figure 4F), superoxide dismutase (SOD; Figure 4G), cholesterol (Figure 4H), LDL (Figure 4I), and HDL (Figure 4J), was reanalyzed. The corresponding RBV values were estimated at 459 (−121 to 1040%), 215 (−65 to 495%), 184 (−80 to 448%), 115 (−16 to 246%), 217 (−18 to 451%), 161 (−73 to 395%), 216 (−208 to 640%), and 224% (−23 to 472%), respectively. The corresponding common intercepts were calculated as 1796 (SE = 51.10; _R_ 2 = 0.96), 2.184 (SE = 0.12; _R_ 2 = 0.78), 37.88 (SE = 0.444; _R_ 2 = 0.70), 146.3 (SE = 4.05, _R_ 2 = 0.62), 52.61 (SE = 0.789; _R_ 2 = 0.84), 156 (SE = 8.85; _R_ 2 = 0.61), 57.13 (SE = 6.42; _R_ 2 = 0.62), and 75.35 (SE = 2.24; _R_ 2 = 0.85), respectively. One-sample _t_-tests demonstrated that the calculated RBV of Zn-Thr reported by Farhadi Javid et al. [14] for body weight gain (_P_ = 0.029) and SOD (_P_ = 0.037) were significantly underestimated. Additionally, the RBV reported for FCR (_P_ = 0.067), cholesterol (_P_ = 0.064), LDL (_P_ = 0.095), HDL (_P_ = 0.069), and ash in tibia (_P_ = 0.051) may have tended to underestimate those of the estimated RBV in this study. However, the calculated RBV by Farhadi Javid et al. [14] for the Zn content of the tibia was overestimated (_P_ = 0.007) compared with the estimated RBV in the present study.

# Multivariate statistical regression analysis and relative quantification based on dimensional-reduction method to compare the taste-active components of different chicken breeds

[*Food Production, Processing and Nutrition*](/)
**volume 6**, Article number: 23 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1028 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6/metrics)

## Abstract

To compare the difference of non-volatile taste-active flavor components of different chicken breeds, four Chinese native yellow feather broilers including Langshan chicken, Chongren chicken, Luyuan chicken and Wenchang chicken were used in the experiment. The contents of free amino acids, 5'-nucleotides and minerals were determined by standard method, and then five principal components were extracted from the multi-index system based on the principal component analysis (PCA). Combined with the Mahalanobis distance analysis method and sensory evaluation results, the advantages and characteristics of each chicken breed were evaluated. The results showed that different kinds of chicken had their own advantages in different evaluation dimensions. The Equivalent umami concentration (ECU) of Wenchang chicken, which had the highest content of amino acids, was 12 g monosodium glutamate (MSG)/100 g, indicating the umami taste of it was very intense. The indexes of Langshan chicken were relatively uniform, with slightly higher mineral content, its overall Mahalanobis distance score was more similar to the "best standard". According to the Mahalanobis distance score, although the difference in amino acid content among each species was the largest, the overall score was more affected by the content of minerals and nucleotides, and there was interaction between each nutrient, which had an impact on the overall Mahalanobis distance score. The sensory evaluation results indicated that Wenchang chicken was the most superior among the taste of the four varieties investigated. Finally, taste compounds affecting the difference of chicken varieties were analyzed by partial least squares regression (PLS), resulting in order of mineral (Ca2+, Mg2+, PO43−) > nucleotide (AMP, IMP) > amino acid. This could provide a theoretical basis for quantitative oriented flavor processing and consumer choice of chicken.

## Introduction

Meat flavor, the combination of taste and order, is an important sensory indicator involved in meat purchasing decision of consumers (Zhuang et al., [2016](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR58 "Zhuang, K., Wu, N., Wang, X., Wu, X., Wang, S., Long, X., & Wei, X. (2016). Effects of 3 feeding modes on the volatile and nonvolatile compounds in the edible tissues of female Chinese mitten crab (Eriocheir sinensis): Feeding modes of female Chinese mitten crab. Journal of Food Science,81(4), S968–S981. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/1750-3841.13229
                  
                ")). The taste-related compounds refer to water-soluble compounds with relatively low molecular weight, including 5'-nucleotides, free amino acids (FAA), organic acids, sugars and inorganic ions (Jayasena et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR9 "Jayasena, D. D., Jung, S., Kim, H. J., Yong, H. I., Nam, K. C., & Jo, C. (2015). Taste-active compound levels in Korean native chicken meat: The effects of bird age and the cooking process. Poultry Science,94(8), 1964–1972. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3382/ps/pev154
                  
                ")). Aroma is related to volatile substances such as hydrocarbons, aldehydes, ketones, esters, alcohols, acids, sulfur and nitrogen compounds (Regueiro et al., [2017](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR25 "Regueiro, J., Negreira, N., & Simal-Gandara, J. (2017). Challenges in relating concentrations of aromas and tastes with flavor features of foods. Critical Reviews in Food Science and Nutrition,57(10), 2112–2127. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/10408398.2015.1048775
                  
                "); Takakura et al., [2014](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR35 "Takakura, Y., Osanai, H., Masuzawa, T., Wakabayashi, H., & Nishimura, T. (2014). Characterization of the key aroma compounds in pork soup stock by using an aroma extract dilution analysis. Bioscience Biotechnology and Biochemistry,78(1), 124–129. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09168451.2014.877184
                  
                ")). There are a number of factors that affect the quality and flavor of chicken, which has been reported by many researchers (Dashdorj et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR4 "Dang, Y., Gao, X., Ma, F., & Wu, X. (2015). Comparison of umami taste peptides in water-soluble extractions of Jinhua and Parma hams. LWT - Food Science and Technology,60(2), 1179–1186. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lwt.2014.09.014
                  
                "); Jayasena et al., [2013](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR8 "Jayasena, D. D., Ahn, D. U., Nam, K. C., & Jo, C. (2013). Flavour chemistry of chicken meat: A eeview. Asian-Australasian Journal of Animal Sciences,26(5), 732–742. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5713/ajas.2012.12619
                  
                "); H. Li et al., [2016](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR14 "Li, H., Li, X., Zhang, C., Wang, J., Tang, C., & Chen, L. (2016). Flavor compounds and sensory profiles of a novel Chinese marinated chicken. Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture,96(5), 1618–1626. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/jsfa.7263
                  
                "); Qi et al., [2017a](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR22 "Qi, J., Liu, D., Zhou, G., & Xu, X. (2017). Characteristic flavor of traditional soup made by stewing Chinese yellow-feather chickens: Characteristic flavor of traditional. Journal of Food Science,82(9), 2031–2040. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/1750-3841.13801
                  
                "), [2017b](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR23 "Qi, J., Wang, H., Zhou, G., Xu, X., Li, X., Bai, Y., & Yu, X. (2017). Evaluation of the taste-active and volatile compounds in stewed meat from the Chinese yellow-feather chicken breed. International Journal of Food Properties,20(sup3), S2579–S2595. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/10942912.2017.1375514
                  
                ")). From the macro level, the main influencing factors are breed, age, feeding method, diet composition, etc. From the micro level, the diameter, quantity and type of muscle fibers have an important impact on the texture, while the content of intramuscular fat, inosinic acid and thiamine can significantly affect the tenderness, flavor and juiciness of meat (Yue et al., [2016](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR50 "Yue, J., Zhang, Y., Jin, Y., Deng, Y., & Zhao, Y. (2016). Impact of high hydrostatic pressure on non-volatile and volatile compounds of squid muscles. Food Chemistry,194, 12–19. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2015.07.134
                  
                ")). Generally, the macro level determines the micro level. The flavor precursors of chicken, such as fatty acids, amino acids, reducing sugars, will accumulate with the increase of age and feed. Most of them participate in lipid oxidation reaction, Maillard reaction, amino acid degradation and other biochemical reactions, thereby affecting the flavor of broilers (Jayasena et al., [2013](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR8 "Jayasena, D. D., Ahn, D. U., Nam, K. C., & Jo, C. (2013). Flavour chemistry of chicken meat: A eeview. Asian-Australasian Journal of Animal Sciences,26(5), 732–742. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5713/ajas.2012.12619
                  
                ")). For example, Takahashi et al. ([2012](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR34 "Takahashi, H., Rikimaru, K., Kiyohara, R., & Yamaguchi, S. (2012). Effect of arachidonic acid-enriched oil diet supplementation on the taste of broiler meat. Asian-Australasian Journal of Animal Sciences,25(6), 845–851. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5713/ajas.2011.11517
                  
                ")) found that dietary arachidonic acid was significantly positively correlated with the content of volatile flavor substances and taste characteristics of umami and aftertaste in chicken. S. Zhang et al. ([2022](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR54 "Zhang, X., Xu, H. P., An, X. Y., Geng, H., & Zhang, S. M. (2015). Discussion on fault degree assessment method for steady process of liquid propellant rocket engine based on Mahalanobis distance. Computer Measurement & Control,23(08), 2745–2748. 
                  https://doi.org/10.16526/j.cnki.11-4762/tp.2015.08.041
                  
                ")) explored the difference in nutritional flavor of Lueyang Black-bone chicken muscles at different ages, predicted that hens should be listed between 120 and 150 days of age, and roosters should be listed at 120 days of age. Therefore, it is more convenient to compare the deep differences between different chicken breeds under the same age and breeding conditions. In this study, the age and breeding conditions of different broilers were controlled to make the same starting point for research.

Compared with aroma compounds, taste compounds are low molecular weight water-soluble compounds having no volatility or lower volatility (Dang et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR3 "Chen, T., Tan, Y., Guo, L., Liu, Q., Liu, A., Hu, X., & Peng, T. (2003). Analysis on mineral contents in muscle, heart, liver and egg of Hyline chicken at different feed condition. Progress in Veterinary Medicine,05, 81–83. 
                  https://doi.org/10.16437/j.cnki.1007-5038.2003.05.027
                  
                ")). Non-volatile taste constituents mainly include free amino acids, small molecular peptides hydrolyzed by muscle protein, 5'-nucleotides degraded by adenosine triphosphate (ATP), inorganic salts and organic acids (Hajeb & Jinap, [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR7 "Hajeb, P., & Jinap, S. (2015). Umami taste components and their sources in Asian foods. Critical Reviews in Food Science and Nutrition,55(6), 778–791. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/10408398.2012.678422
                  
                ")). The basic taste categories are composed of sweet, sour, bitter, salty and umami, which can be induced by these taste-active compounds substances when dissolved in saliva and bound to receptor proteins on the tongue (Simon et al., [2006](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR32 "Simon, S. A., de Araujo, I. E., Gutierrez, R., & Nicolelis, M. A. L. (2006). The neural mechanisms of gustation: A distributed processing code. Nature Reviews Neuroscience,7(11), 890–901. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/nrn2006
                  
                ")). Taste perception is one of the most complex human behaviors. It involves almost all senses and is closely related to olfactory perception. The odor image generated by the olfactory pathway participates in the formation of taste perception (Shepherd, [2006](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR29 "Shepherd, G. M. (2006). Smell images and the flavour system in the human brain. Nature,444(7117), 316–321. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/nature05405
                  
                ")). Although from a physiological point of view, order and taste receptors as well as neural pathways leading to the brain are separated, the flavor of food perceived through oral processing is produced by the joint action of aroma and taste substances that are recognized by the brain (Papies et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR21 "Papies, E. K., Claassen, M. A., Rusz, D., & Best, M. (2022). Flavors of desire: Cognitive representations of appetitive stimuli and their motivational implications. Journal of experimental psychology-general,151(8), 1919–1941. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0001157
                  
                "); Serrano-Gonzalez et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR28 "Serrano-Gonzalez, M., Herting, M. M., Lim, S. L., Sullivan, N. J., Kim, R., Espinoza, J., Koppin, C. M., Javier, J. R., Kim, M. S., & Luo, S. (2021). Developmental changes in food perception and preference. Frontiers in psychology,12, 654200.")). Therefore, the perception of chicken flavor is the result of the complementary perception of smell and taste.

Yellow feather broilers are Chinese native chickens which have a longer growth cycle, firmer meat quality and stronger chicken flavor than white-feathered chickens (Qi et al., [2017a](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR22 "Qi, J., Liu, D., Zhou, G., & Xu, X. (2017). Characteristic flavor of traditional soup made by stewing Chinese yellow-feather chickens: Characteristic flavor of traditional. Journal of Food Science,82(9), 2031–2040. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/1750-3841.13801
                  
                "), [2017b](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR23 "Qi, J., Wang, H., Zhou, G., Xu, X., Li, X., Bai, Y., & Yu, X. (2017). Evaluation of the taste-active and volatile compounds in stewed meat from the Chinese yellow-feather chicken breed. International Journal of Food Properties,20(sup3), S2579–S2595. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/10942912.2017.1375514
                  
                ")). They can be divided into three major types: fast growth (50–80 days), medium growth (70–100 days) and slow growth (over 100 days) according to the industry sources. The slower the growth rate, the longer the age, the higher contents of inosine 5′-monophosphate (IMP) (Xu et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR45 "Xu, N., Ye, J., Li, L., Wang, X., Wang, P., Han, M., & Xu, X. (2021). Exploration of flavor and taste of soft-boiled chicken at different post-mortem aging time: Based on GC-IMS and multivariate statistical analysis. Food Bioscience,43, 101326. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fbio.2021.101326
                  
                ")). It is of important significance for the independent development of broiler industry to select high-quality yellow feather broilers for breeding. Mahalanobis distance is a method proposed by Indian statistician Mahalanobis in 1936 (Mahalanobis, [1936](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR17 "Mahalanobis, P. C. (1936). On the generalized distance in statistics. proceedings of the national institute of sciences, 2(1), 49–55. 
                  http://www.researchgate.net/publication/240311080_On_the_Generalized_Distance_in_Statistics
                  
                ")) that can effectively calculate the closest distance between a sample and the "center of gravity" of a sample set or the similarity of two unknown sample sets (X. Zhang et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR52 "Zhang, M., Karangwa, E., Duhoranimana, E., Zhang, X., Xia, S., Yu, J., & Xu, M. (2017). Characterization of pork bone soup odor active compounds from traditional clay and commercial electrical stewpots by sensory evaluation, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry/olfactometry and partial least squares regression. Flavour and Fragrance Journal,32(6), 470–483. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/ffj.3406
                  
                ")). Compared with the Euclidean distance, it is not affected by the dimension, which means that the Mahalanobis distance between two points is not related to the measurement unit of the original data (Ramos-Guajardo & Ferraro, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR24 "Ramos-Guajardo, A. B., & Ferraro, M. B. (2020). A fuzzy clustering approach for fuzzy data based on a generalized distance. Fuzzy Sets and Systems,389, 29–50. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fss.2019.09.010
                  
                ")). Sensory evaluation is one of the best methods to measure product quality which can apply food theory to practice and simulate consumers liking even towards products (H. Li et al., [2016](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR14 "Li, H., Li, X., Zhang, C., Wang, J., Tang, C., & Chen, L. (2016). Flavor compounds and sensory profiles of a novel Chinese marinated chicken. Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture,96(5), 1618–1626. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/jsfa.7263
                  
                "); Zhao & Chen, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR55 "Zhao, J., & Chen, J. (2021). Detecting maturity in fresh Lycium barbarum L. fruit using color information. Horticulturae,7(5), 108. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/horticulturae7050108
                  
                ")). Equivalent umami concentration (EUC) is related to the content of amino acids (glutamic acid and aspartic acid, Glu and Asp) and 5'-nucleotides (AMP and GMP) in the food system (W. Wang et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR37 "Wang, H., Wang, P., Yu, J., Zeng, X., Li, Z., Shu, J., Zou, J., & Xu, X. (2021). Sensory evaluation combined with principal component analysis and Mahalanobis distance to compare the volatile flavor substances of different chicken species. Science and Technology of Food Industry,42(17), 235–240. 
                  https://doi.org/10.13386/j.issn1002-0306.2020100031
                  
                ")). The umami intensity provided by them is converted into the concentration of sodium salt (g/100 g) glutamine with the same freshness, which can reflect the comprehensive influence of different dimensions on the umami taste of chicken (D. Chen & Zhang, [2007](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR6 "GB/T 1629.2–2012 (2012) General guidelines for selection, training and managemtns of evaluators. Beijing: Standards press of China.")).

The volatile compounds of four representative yellow feather broilers have been analyzed from multiple dimensions and published (H. Wang et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR42 "Wang, Y., Liu, Y., Cai, S., Liu, Y., Liu, J., Liu, X., Liu, S., & Kong, L. (2012). Effects of leaves and seeds of S.album on meat quality of Wenchang chicken. China Animal Husbandry & Veterinary Medicine, 39(12), 64–69. 
                  https://kns.cnki.net/kcms/detail/detail.aspx?FileName=GWXK201212016&DbName=CJFQ2012
                  
                ")). Both the principal component analysis and the Mahalanobis distance analysis method were used. Combined with the evaluation results of a professionally trained sensory evaluation panels, this article reveals the formation of non-volatile taste-active components of yellow feather broilers.

## Material and methods

### Reagents

All chemicals and solutions are analytical grade. All standard substances (amino acids, nucleotides, mineral elements) and methanol were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). In addition, perchloric acid, n-hexane, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, hydrogen peroxide, and nitric acid were purchased from Sinopharm (Shanghai, China).

### Sample preparation

Whole chickens about four broilers (Langshan chicken, Chongren chicken, Luyuan chicken, Wenchang chicken) whose average weight is about 1.5 kg, were provided by the Yangzhou Poultry Research Institute of the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, with the same age and feeding conditions. There were still some differences among individuals for yellow feather chicken of the same breed. But if the sample capacity was too large, the experiment cannot proceed smoothly, so the sample size has certain limitations. In order to reduce individual differences, we selected 5 chickens from each breed, mixed them, and each experiment was in triplicate. Totally Twenty hens were removed from the production line after the process of hanging-electric-stunning-slaughtering-draining-defeathering (Jiangsu Lihua Co., Ltd, Changzhou, China). The breast and thigh muscles were quickly picked, vacuum packed in polyethylene vacuum bags and placed in -30 °C freezer for air-cooled quick freezing. After 1 h, the samples were transported to the laboratory in the incubator with ice bags and stored in the freezer at -18 °C.

Chicken cooking procedure: One liter of purified water was added to the pot and heated to 96–99 °C, at which time the water is slightly boiling. The thigh and breast of the same chicken that has been thawed at room temperature of 25 °C for two hours were added to the slightly boiling water bath for 20 min by an induction cooker (C21-SC011, Joyang Co., Ltd., Jinan, China).

Meat sample preparation: The cooked chicken breast and thigh were mixed according to the weight ratio of 1:1, and then crushed and blended at 300 W for 20 s with a meat grinder (J10, Oaks, Guangzhou, China), after which the extraction of free amino acids and 5'-nucleotides components as well as sensory evaluation took place.

### Free amino acid (FAA) analysis

Four grams of chicken samples were homogenized and extracted with 20 mL 3% (w/v) 5-sulfosalicylic acid dihydrate in a 50 mL centrifuge tube at 10,000 rpm (three times, 20 s for each) using an Ultra-Turrax macerator (T-25, IKA, Staufen, Germany) in an ice bath. The homogenate was centrifuged at 12,096 g for 15 min at 4 °C in a centrifuge (Avanti J-26XP, Beckman, Shanghai, China) and then the supernatant was shaken with 2 mL of n-hexane (Jo et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR10 "Jo, Y., An, K. A., Arshad, M. S., & Kwon, J. H. (2018). Effects of e-beam irradiation on amino acids, fatty acids, and volatiles of smoked duck meat during storage. Innovative Food Science & Emerging Technologies,47, 101–109. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ifset.2017.12.008
                  
                ")). The aqueous phase was filtered through a 0.45-μm membrane filter after the organic layer was removed, then free amino acids were analyzed on an amino acid automatic analyzer (L-8800, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) which was equipped with BioBasic SCX cation exchange column (4.6 mm × 60 mm, 5 μm) and UV detector. Each amino acid was identified by matching its retention time with that of an authentic standard in the L-8800, and quantified using peak areas of the respective amino acid standard.

### Nucleotide analysis

LC-16 High Performance Liquid Chromatography (Shimadzu Co., LTD, Japan) with 2998 PDA detector was used to analyze tested nucleotides in chicken. The tested nucleotides including 5'-guanylic acid (GMP), 5'-inosinic acid (IMP), 5'-adenosine acid (AMP), hypoxanthine (Hx) and inosine, were separated by X Bridge C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm) purchased from Agela Technologies (Shanghai, China). The temperature was controlled at 25 °C. The eluents used were as follows: mobile phase A is 0.05 mol/L potassium dihydrogen phosphate buffer (pH 4.5), while mobile phase B is methanol. To achieve separation of the nucleotides, the flow rate was set to 1.0 mL/min and the following gradient was performed: initial of 98% A for 14 min, linear change to 85% A for at 15 min, hold for 7 min and finely linear change to 98% A for 9 min. The quantification of nucleotides is determined by external standard method (Qi et al., [2017a](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR22 "Qi, J., Liu, D., Zhou, G., & Xu, X. (2017). Characteristic flavor of traditional soup made by stewing Chinese yellow-feather chickens: Characteristic flavor of traditional. Journal of Food Science,82(9), 2031–2040.

### Determination of mineral elements

The concentrations of mineral elements (Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, and PO43−) were determined by ICP-MS (ICAP RQ, Thermo Fisher, USA). A microwave digestion system (TOPEX+, PreeKem Scientific Instruments Co. Ltd., China) was used for acid digestion of the chicken samples. 5 ml concentrated nitric acid and 0.2 g chicken sample were added into the digestion tube. Digestion is carried out according to the procedure in Table [1](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#Tab1) thereafter the tube placed into the microwave digestion apparatus. After the microwave digestion procedure, the samples were digested to a clear solution. The digested solution was transferred to plastic tubes and diluted to 5% of an acid concentration.

### Taste activity value (TAV)

TAV was defined as the ratio between the concentration of a substance determined in the samples to the taste threshold value of it (J. Zheng et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR56 "Zheng, J., Tao, N., Gong, J., Gu, S. Q., & Xu, C. (2015). Comparison of non-volatile taste-active compounds between the cooked meats of pre- and post-spawning Yangtze Coilia ectenes. Fisheries Science,81(3), 559–568. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s12562-015-0858-7
                  
                ")). TAV can reflect the contribution of a single compound to the overall taste: when TAV is less than 1, the substance does not contribute or contributes little to the taste; when TAV is more than 1, the substance contributes significantly to the taste, the larger the value, the greater the contribution (Khan et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s43014-023-00204-6#ref-CR12 "Khan, M. I., Jo, C., & Tariq, M. R. (2015). Meat flavor precursors and factors influencing flavor precursors—A systematic review. Meat Science,110, 278–284.

### The principal component analysis (PCA) and Mahalanobis distance

The principal component analysis (PCA) process can map high-dimensional features to low-dimensional feature spaces, thereby constructing brand-new orthogonal features to eliminate many overlapping information. The principal components and scores of multiple variables which were compressed can be obtained. Food Chemistry,104(3), 1200–1205.

                ")).
